nodes.name: Fictional Detectives
nodes.description: Famous fictional detectives and their creators

items.prompt: Which author created Sherlock Holmes, the brilliant detective of 221B Baker Street who solves cases through observation and deduction?
items.explanation:
Holmes first appeared in *A Study in Scarlet* (1887); Doyle wrote 4 novels and 56 short stories featuring Holmes
Holmes was partly modeled on Dr. Joseph Bell, a diagnostician whose observational skills Doyle admired as a medical student
Doyle grew so tired of Holmes that he killed him off at Reichenbach Falls in 1893 — public outcry forced a revival in 1903
'Elementary, my dear Watson' — Holmes never says this exact phrase in any Doyle story
Holmes is the most portrayed fictional human character in film and TV history, with over 70 actors in the role
items.cardFront: Sherlock Holmes (Baker Street, deduction, Dr. Watson)

items.prompt: Which author created Hercule Poirot, the Belgian detective famous for his 'little grey cells' and fastidious mustache?
items.explanation:
Poirot appeared in 33 novels and 54 short stories from 1920 (*The Mysterious Affair at Styles*) to 1975 (*Curtain*)
Christie wrote *Curtain* during WWII as insurance in case she died; it was published only in the year she died
Poirot's death in *Curtain* was announced on the front page of the *New York Times* — one of few fictional characters so honored
David Suchet played Poirot on television from 1989 to 2013, completing all the stories — a 25-year commitment
Christie also created Miss Marple; together they make her the best-selling mystery writer of all time

items.prompt: Which author created Philip Marlowe, the hard-boiled Los Angeles private eye who appeared in *The Big Sleep* and *Farewell, My Lovely*?
items.explanation:
Marlowe appeared in seven novels from *The Big Sleep* (1939) to *Playback* (1958)
Chandler also wrote the original *Double Indemnity* screenplay (1944) with Billy Wilder — one of noir's defining films
Marlowe's wisecracking, morally upright loner defined the hard-boiled private detective archetype
Humphrey Bogart's portrayal in the 1946 *The Big Sleep* is definitive; the film's plot is notoriously impossible to follow
Chandler began writing crime fiction at age 44 after losing his job as an oil executive during the Depression
items.cardFront: Philip Marlowe (hard-boiled, Los Angeles, *The Big Sleep*)

items.prompt: Which author created Sam Spade, the San Francisco private detective who investigates the Maltese Falcon?
items.explanation:
Spade appeared only in *The Maltese Falcon* (1930) and three short stories
Hammett worked as a Pinkerton Agency detective before turning to fiction — his crime writing drew on real experience
Humphrey Bogart played Spade in the 1941 John Huston film — Bogart's breakthrough as a leading man
Hammett also created Nick and Nora Charles (*The Thin Man*, 1934) and the Continental Op
Hammett refused to name names before the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1951; served six months in prison
items.cardFront: Sam Spade (San Francisco P.I., *The Maltese Falcon*)

items.prompt: Which author created Miss Jane Marple, the elderly amateur sleuth from the village of St. Mary Mead who solves murders through her knowledge of human nature?
items.explanation:
Miss Marple appeared in 12 novels from *Murder at the Vicarage* (1930) to *Sleeping Murder* (published 1976)
Marple uses her intimate knowledge of village life — and its vices — to decode human behavior everywhere she goes
Christie preferred Poirot but felt Marple was closer to the detective tradition of the 'intuitive amateur'
Joan Hickson played Marple in a BBC series (1984–1992), considered by Christie's estate the definitive version
Christie is the best-selling mystery writer of all time; her works outsell every author except the Bible and Shakespeare
items.cardFront: Miss Marple (elderly amateur, St. Mary Mead, human nature)

items.prompt: Which author created Nero Wolfe, the obese, orchid-growing New York detective who rarely leaves his brownstone and relies on his legman Archie Goodwin?
items.explanation:
Wolfe appeared in 33 novels and 39 novellas from *Fer-de-Lance* (1934) to *A Family Affair* (1975)
Wolfe weighs 'one-seventh of a ton,' grows orchids on his penthouse roof, and refuses to leave his West 35th Street brownstone except in extremity
Archie Goodwin is the first-person narrator — urbane, witty, and a foil to Wolfe's sedentary brilliance
Stout was a founding member of the Authors Guild and a prominent anti-fascist activist during WWII
Timothy Hutton played Archie Goodwin in the A&E series (2001–2002)
items.cardFront: Nero Wolfe (obese, orchids, brownstone, Archie Goodwin)

items.prompt: Which author created Lord Peter Wimsey, the aristocratic amateur detective and WWI veteran who solves crimes between collecting incunabula and making cricket puns?
items.explanation:
Wimsey appeared in 11 novels from *Whose Body?* (1923) to *Busman's Honeymoon* (1937)
Sayers also wrote important scholarly work on medieval literature, including translations of Dante's *Divine Comedy*
Harriet Vane, introduced in *Strong Poison* (1930) as a murder suspect, eventually becomes Wimsey's wife
Sayers is credited with elevating the detective novel from puzzle to serious fiction
Wimsey was partly modeled on Sayers's friend Eric Whelpton; she later said she 'fell in love with him' during writing
items.cardFront: Lord Peter Wimsey (aristocratic, WWI vet, amateur detective)

items.prompt: Which author created Inspector Morse, the crossword-solving, Wagnerian-opera-loving Oxford detective whose full first name is only revealed in his final novel?
items.explanation:
Morse appeared in 13 novels from *Last Bus to Woodstock* (1975) to *The Remorseful Day* (1999)
Morse's first name — Endeavour — is revealed only in *The Wench Is Dead* (1989); Dexter kept it secret for years
John Thaw played Morse in the ITV series (1987–2000); a prequel series *Endeavour* ran 2012–2023
Morse dies at the end of *The Remorseful Day*, just as Dexter was diagnosed with a serious illness
Dexter himself made Hitchcock-style cameo appearances in many episodes of the TV series

items.prompt: Which author created Dr. Kay Scarpetta, the forensic pathologist and Chief Medical Examiner who became the template for crime procedural heroes?
items.explanation:
Scarpetta first appeared in *Postmortem* (1990) — the first novel to win the Edgar, Creasey, Anthony, and Macavity Awards
Scarpetta's forensic detail was groundbreaking — Cornwell worked in a real medical examiner's office for research
Helped launch the 1990s boom in forensic crime fiction that eventually spawned the *CSI* TV franchise
Cornwell has published 25+ Scarpetta novels; Scarpetta is based partly in Virginia, later Miami and New York
Cornwell controversially spent $7 million trying to prove that Walter Sickert was Jack the Ripper (2002)
items.cardFront: Kay Scarpetta (forensic pathologist, Chief Medical Examiner)

items.prompt: Which author created Kinsey Millhone, the twice-divorced Santa Teresa private eye whose cases form the alphabetically titled 'alphabet series' from *A is for Alibi* to *Y is for Yesterday*?
items.explanation:
Millhone appeared in 25 novels from *A is for Alibi* (1982) to *Y is for Yesterday* (2017); Grafton died before completing *Z*
Grafton set the series in fictional 'Santa Teresa' (based on Santa Barbara, California) in the 1980s — deliberately avoiding technology
Millhone lives in a converted garage apartment, drives an old VW Beetle, and eats peanut butter on crackers
Grafton said the 'A' in 'A is for Alibi' was inspired by anger at her ex-husband during a custody dispute — she imagined killing him 14 ways
One of the 'three queens' of American crime fiction alongside Sara Paretsky and Marcia Muller
items.cardFront: Kinsey Millhone (Santa Teresa P.I., alphabet series)

items.prompt: Which author created V.I. Warshawski, the Chicago feminist private detective who helped define the feminist crime novel from *Indemnity Only* onward?
items.explanation:
V.I. (Victoria Iphigenia) Warshawski appeared in 20+ novels from *Indemnity Only* (1982) onward
A groundbreaking feminist detective who operates in working-class Chicago, often clashing with corrupt institutions
Paretsky co-founded Sisters in Crime (1986), an organization to promote women crime writers
Kathleen Turner played Warshawski in the 1991 film adaptation
One of the 'three queens' alongside Sue Grafton and Marcia Muller; all three debuted feminist P.I. novels in 1982

items.prompt: Which author created Easy Rawlins, the reluctant Black private eye in post-WWII Los Angeles whose cases are set against real events of African American history?
items.explanation:
Rawlins first appeared in *Devil in a Blue Dress* (1990); Mosley has written 15 Easy Rawlins novels
Set in the Watts neighborhood of Los Angeles; each novel covers a specific year from 1948 to the 1960s
Bill Clinton cited *Devil in a Blue Dress* as a favorite novel during his 1992 presidential campaign, boosting Mosley's career
Denzel Washington played Rawlins in the 1995 Carl Franklin film adaptation
Mosley received the PEN USA's Lifetime Achievement Award and the Grand Master Award from the Mystery Writers of America
items.cardFront: Easy Rawlins (Black P.I., postwar Los Angeles, *Devil in a Blue Dress*)
